text: Welcome to This is My Architecture. Today we're talking automated drift detection and I'm joined

solving? Fugue is trying to solve this business problem where people are not building infrastructure
the right secure compliant way up front. With Fugue you do that and also make sure that
the system will always enforce your configuration. So you're allowing users to use your own library
and their libraries of kind of a domain specific language around things like GDPR and other

to provision infrastructure through it using the compliance libraries up front and once
it builds that infrastructure then it manages it for you moving forward. So it's not just
building it it's actually checking it as well. Correct. Yep. And that's a big business

looking at is what we call as the conductor which is the orchestration engine. This is the very

instance and it sits as part of an auto scaling group of min-max 1.1. The persistence layer is
actually in the DynamoDB table. So we're following the same best architecture practices by AWS.
So that's talking into there all the time. Correct. Yep. So this is the persistence layer. We use SQS
that we use SQS queueing system to actually communicate from the outside world for anyone over here.
So people will send SQS messages for anything that they need from the conductor. And that will then

standards. Right. So once the EC2 instance gets the SQS messaging it actually does authentication using IAMS.
And STS and KMS behind the scene. SQS and SNS are the brains of everything from the outside world.
And all the notification that the conductor needs from within. So everything is flowing through those

is by design. Our founders came from national security backgrounds. So we ensure that whatever we

So the S3 bucket which is close to the public and only the EC2 instance or the conductor can access is
storing kind of your map of your infrastructure declaration as well as the policies. And we spoke
about the standard policies like CIS, GDPR, HIPAA, NIST 853 compliance, PCI. FUG also allows you to build
your own policy using policy as code approach in the same domain language that you declare infrastructure with FUG.
And all of that is compiled and the compile code is uploaded to the S3 bucket. And that's what the
EC2 instance uses to download and start building infrastructure. And because it's compiled you're
eliminating the errors up front. Correct. The compiler not just takes care of syntactical errors. With FUG,

our baseline libraries also then make sure that the declaration are actually correct so you don't have to deal with rollbacks once it starts building the infrastructure and the compliance. Now this is not running in a FUG

account. This is running in the customers account. Is that right? That is correct. And yeah, it's not a SAS application. It's not hosted by FUG. This gets deployed in one command in a customer's AWS account. In one AWS account that they have. And then using cross-account IAM role, the conductor then manages infrastructure in other AWS accounts. And over here we just have two, but it can be as many AWS accounts as you have.
And just so I'm understanding this, this is not just detecting that a drift has taken place or some issue has taken place. It's rectifying as well.

Correct. Yeah. So once it builds infrastructure, what it does next is actually scan it on a regular interval that can be changed as per your setting. By default, it's every 60 seconds. It will scan for the infrastructure configuration, map it to the original declared state that you provided. And if it comes across any drift, it will not only use the SNS topic to notify you in real time, but by default, it will also
return it to the good baseline, secure compliant baseline that you provided.
